sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS manual defines 561312 around client-specific executive and senior-management search, recruitment, and placement; it lists strategy, specification, research, identification, screening, interviewing, qualification verification, offer negotiation, and assimilation, and supplies the cited cross-references.

The May 2023 BLS table supports the stated broader NAICS 561300 occupational data, including management, human-resources, computer, sales, and office-administrative occupations. It does not isolate 561312 and is visibly dominated by occupations associated with temporary help, exactly as the packet discloses.

SHRM reports 51% recruiting use, the listed recruiting applications, 89% reporting time or efficiency gains, and a continuing role for human judgment. Its unweighted sample covers 2,040 HR professionals across U.S. organizations, not executive-search providers, as disclosed in the bridge.
The 2026 SHRM policy document reports planned five-year expansion of AI use in recruiting at 40% and repeats the 51% adoption and 89% efficiency figures while emphasizing human decision-making.

The 2024 Heidrick & Struggles 10-K supports the retained-search workflow, compensation-linked fees of about one-third of first-year compensation, three-increment billing, recurring engagements, technology and insourcing risks, 4.9% executive-search revenue growth, and 0.1% confirmation growth. It is a large global retained-search firm rather than an LMM boutique, a limitation the packet repeatedly discloses.

Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned within five years to sell, transfer, or take public their businesses. This is an all-industry intention measure rather than completed qualifying control transfers, matching the stated proxy bridge and caveat.

The December 2025 8-K confirms completion of the merger that made Heidrick & Struggles a wholly owned subsidiary of the buyer. It supports only the packet's directional exact-industry transferability signal, not an LMM transfer rate.
The BLS 2024-2034 industry table reports a 2.5% compound annual real-output growth projection for broader NAICS 561300. The source does not isolate executive search, as the packet discloses.

caveats: The a and d5 anchors use broader NAICS 561300 evidence that is heavily contaminated by temporary-help activity; the exact-industry values therefore remain judgmental despite plausible and explicit bridges.
The rho evidence measures unweighted U.S. employer-HR adoption and reported benefits rather than implemented workflow coverage, verified labor removal, or adoption among executive-search providers.

The s5 base maps a 22% all-industry owner intention rate and one very large public-company merger into LMM qualifying transfer probability; completion, control-transfer, size, and key-person differences make this the most consequential inferential weakness.

The q, workflow, and firm-behavior evidence relies heavily on one large global retained-search company and does not establish the contract mix, pricing power, AI benefit sharing, or relationship transferability of LMM boutiques.
summary: Publishable with caveats. Mechanics pass, all eight primitive-cited sources are reachable, and the cited factual claims are supported. The default lens is coherent, evidence states and proxy bridges are candid, factor semantics are not reversed or double counted, and the narrative follows the evidence. The record remains model-conditioned because several base values translate broad employer, employment-services, owner-intention, and large-company evidence into LMM executive-search assumptions; those limitations are material to diligence but do not constitute a material review finding under the frozen test because no identified source failure or dishonest evidence downgrade is shown to change the base tier or action category.
